title: Building a Production RAG Pipeline Under $20/Month
excerpt: A step-by-step tutorial that shows exactly how to build and ship building a production rag pipeline under $20/month with practical defaults. You’ll get a clear architecture, implementation steps, and the trade-offs that matter in production (covering RAG, Llama 3, PgVector).
contentHtml:
What you’re building

This guide walks through a small, reliable RAG stack you can run on a cheap VPS (or even a home server): a crawler/ingestion step, embeddings, a vector store (Postgres + pgvector), and an API that answers questions with citations.

Architecture (simple and practical)

  • Ingest: collect documents (Markdown, HTML, PDFs).
  • Chunk: split into 500–1000 token-ish chunks with overlap.
  • Embed: generate vectors for each chunk.
  • Store: write chunks + vectors to Postgres + pgvector.
  • Retrieve: similarity search + (optional) rerank.
  • Generate: send retrieved chunks to the LLM with a grounded prompt.

Step 1 — Postgres + pgvector

Use Postgres because it’s cheap, boring, and easy to operate.

-- Enable pgvector
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS documents (
  id bigserial PRIMARY KEY,
  source text NOT NULL,
  title text,
  url text,
  created_at timestamptz DEFAULT now()
);

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS chunks (
  id bigserial PRIMARY KEY,
  document_id bigint REFERENCES documents(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  chunk_index int NOT NULL,
  content text NOT NULL,
  embedding vector(1536),
  metadata jsonb DEFAULT '{}'::jsonb
);

-- Index for fast vector search
CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS chunks_embedding_idx ON chunks
USING ivfflat (embedding vector_cosine_ops) WITH (lists = 100);

Tip: tune lists and run ANALYZE after bulk inserts.

Step 2 — Chunking that doesn’t ruin retrieval

  • Start with 800–1200 characters per chunk (or ~500–800 tokens).
  • Add 10–15% overlap so important context isn’t cut.
  • Store source and url in metadata so you can cite results.

Step 3 — Embeddings

Pick one embeddings model and stick to it. Consistency matters more than squeezing the last 2% of quality.

// pseudo-code
for (chunk of chunks) {
  const embedding = embed(chunk.content)
  insert(chunk, embedding)
}

Step 4 — Retrieval query

Retrieve top k chunks with cosine similarity, then optionally rerank.

SELECT content, metadata
FROM chunks
ORDER BY embedding <=> $1
LIMIT 8;

Step 5 — Grounded generation prompt

Keep the system prompt strict. Ask for citations and refuse to guess when context is missing.

SYSTEM: Answer using ONLY the provided context. If not enough context, say you don't know.

USER: Question: ...

CONTEXT:
- [1] ...
- [2] ...

Cost breakdown (rough)

  • VPS: $5–$10/mo (2GB–4GB RAM is enough for API + Postgres for small sites).
  • Embeddings: depends on volume; keep ingestion incremental and cache.
  • LLM: pay-per-token or self-host. Start with API usage and optimize later.

Operational checklist

  • Log queries + retrieved chunk IDs.
  • Keep a re-ingestion path (docs change).
  • Add basic rate limiting and request size limits.
  • Track “no answer” rate (it’s a quality signal).
